items.q: Does appropriation art automatically infringe copyright?
items.correct: Not necessarily — outcomes depend on the jurisdiction, the use made, and defences such as fair use or fair dealing.
items.misconception: Yes — using any existing image is always illegal.
items.random_wrong: No — copyright never applies to artworks.
items.why: Students assume appropriation equals theft, but courts weigh transformation, purpose and market harm case by case.

items.q: How long does UK copyright in an artistic work generally last?
items.correct: For the creator's lifetime plus 70 years.
items.misconception: Exactly 50 years from the date of creation.
items.random_wrong: Ten years, after which it must be renewed.
items.why: Students confuse older or international terms; the UK standard term is life of the author plus 70 years.

items.q: When Sherrie Levine re-photographed Walker Evans's images, what was the point?
items.correct: To question originality, authorship and ownership in art.
items.misconception: She was copying because she lacked her own ideas.
items.why: Students read appropriation as laziness rather than a deliberate conceptual challenge to ideas of authorship.

items.q: UK copyright lasts how long after the author's death?
items.why: Correct answer: 70 years. “50 years” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Sherrie Levine's most famous re-photographs are 'After':
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Walker Evans. “Ansel Adams” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: If a photographer died in 1955, in what year does their UK copyright expire (70 years after death)?
items.why: Exact answer: 2025. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
